stages.purpose: Open with location/weather small talk, ease the candidate in, and build rapport before substantive questions.
Elicit the candidate's career narrative, hobbies, company knowledge, and motivation (including 'why a startup / why this company').
Probe the hardest/most technical thing built (often outside work), drilling into architecture, ownership, and difficulty to verify genuine technical depth.
Assess agency (hacking a system / breaking rules), competitiveness (and its cost), motivation, and niche expertise/curiosity.
Invite candidate questions, then screen logistics (5-day in-person SF, visa sponsorship) and explain next steps (take-home, technical interviews with CTO Andrew, work trial).

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Where are you calling in from?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: Where are you calling in from?
I was wondering where are you calling in from?
Calling in from?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Immediately after initial greetings/audio check

likely_reasoning.explanation: Standard icebreaker to ease the candidate in; location also doubles as soft context for the in-person SF role.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: How's the weather there? / follow-up location small talk

representative_verbatim_variants.text: How's the weather?
Is it cold?
Is it, is it hot down there?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Candidate states their city/region

likely_reasoning.explanation: Light continuation of small talk to build comfort before substantive questions.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Can you tell me a bit about yourself?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: Can you tell me a bit about yourself?
Just, like, just a little bit about yourself, please.
Can you just tell me a bit about yourself?

common_preceding_contexts.description: After interviewer introduces self and frames the call as personal/non-technical

likely_reasoning.explanation: Open-ended opener to learn the candidate's career arc and seed later probing.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: What do you do for fun / what are your hobbies?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: And what do you do for fun?
And then what about your hobbies? Do you play any sports? Do you play video games?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Following the professional self-introduction

likely_reasoning.explanation: Interviewers repeatedly framed the call as getting to know the candidate as a person; hobbies seed rapport and later niche-knowledge probes.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: What do you know about Weave/WorkWeave?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: For starters, what do you know about Weave?
Can you start by telling me what you know about Weave?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Right after background, before technical deep dive

likely_reasoning.primary_signal_sought: preparation / genuine interest in the company
likely_reasoning.explanation: Gauges how much research the candidate did; notably this question was more common in earlier transcripts and seems to drop out of the later, more refined script.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Why do you want to work at a startup (vs. big tech)?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: Why do you want to work at a startup?
I guess my question is like why not big tech, right? You can make decent enough money, you could have a good work life balance.
why do you want to work at a startup? Like, why not continue your agency? Why not get a nice corporate job that pays a lot of money?

common_preceding_contexts.description: After candidate describes background or current role

likely_reasoning.explanation: Tests whether the candidate genuinely wants startup intensity vs. comfort/money; sometimes posed as a challenge to test conviction.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: What is the hardest thing you've built (technically / outside of work)?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: Can you tell me about a genuinely hard problem you worked on where the final solution was not obvious so like you came up with something clever or elegant.
what is the most technical thing that you have built outside of work?
What is the hardest thing that you've ever built? Technically?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Interviewer explicitly marks 'one technical question' or 'let's dive in'

likely_reasoning.explanation: The single most consistent substantive question; the 'outside of work' framing probes intrinsic motivation and genuine capability beyond assigned tasks.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: What was the hardest part / the moment it got tougher than expected?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: What was the hardest part? Like which specific piece of which workflow was like the hardest part to build?
what was the one moment where you realized like, oh, shit, this is going to be a lot harder than I thought it was?
what was the moment that you realized this problem was hard, like the oh, shit moment

common_preceding_contexts.description: Candidate gives a broad/abstract project description

likely_reasoning.explanation: Drills past surface descriptions to verify the candidate truly engineered something hard rather than routine work.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: What was your specific individual role / were you the only one who built this?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: And what was your role in this? Like were you the one who did everything from design to implementation? ... Very like specifically.
I heard a lot of moving parts there ... What was your main contribution? What team were you on?
And were you the only one on this because you said you'd found this and decided to do it yourself

common_preceding_contexts.description: Candidate describes project using 'we' or lists many components
common_preceding_contexts.frequency_estimate: triggered when candidate uses collective framing

likely_reasoning.explanation: When candidates speak in 'we' or describe many moving parts, the interviewer isolates personal contribution to assess real depth and agency.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: How did you arrive at the solution / why is it not the obvious approach? (technical reasoning probe)

representative_verbatim_variants.text: And how did you end up coming to the solution? Like what prompted you to this?
what would be the obvious solution here? And then why is your solution not the obvious solution?
if the embeddings are the same ... why would the RAG be different if we're doing cosine similarity on a vector DB versus on Pinecone versus on opensearch?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Candidate describes a clever/architectural choice

likely_reasoning.primary_signal_sought: originality of thought / first-principles understanding / honesty under challenge
likely_reasoning.explanation: Tests whether the candidate genuinely understands tradeoffs; in stronger cases the interviewer challenges the premise directly (e.g., cosine similarity, metadata claims) to detect fabrication.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Have you ever applied to YC before?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: Have you applied to yc? Like, yes or no?
Have you ever applied to YC before?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Immediately before asking the 'hacked a non-computer system' question

likely_reasoning.explanation: Functions mainly as a setup to gauge whether the candidate has a rehearsed answer to the signature YC 'hacking' question that follows.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Tell me about a time you hacked a non-computer system / broke the rules to get what you wanted.

representative_verbatim_variants.text: Can you tell me about a time you successfully hacked a non computer system?
can you tell me about the time that you hacked a system to your advantage? Maybe you broke some rules to get what you wanted?
what is a system that you've successfully hacked to your advantage? ... have you broken the rules?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Right after asking if the candidate applied to YC
Interviewer signals 'fun questions' or 'changing lines of questioning'

likely_reasoning.explanation: A signature YC-derived question used to surface scrappy, unconventional problem-solving valued in startup founders/early hires.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Tell me about a time your competitiveness became so intense it cost you something / the most extreme thing you've done to win.

representative_verbatim_variants.text: can you tell me about an area of your life where this competitiveness that you had became so intense that it cost you something?
What is the most insane thing that you have done to win?
What's the most extreme thing that you did to win ... Did it cost you your friendships or something? Did you stay up 36 hours?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Often framed with 'if you want to work at a startup you're competitive by nature'

likely_reasoning.explanation: Assesses competitive intensity (a stated company value) and whether the candidate reflects on the costs of that drive.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: What motivated you to be this competitive? (underlying motivation probe)

representative_verbatim_variants.text: What was the motivation? Did you just want to win? What is it? Was it dopamine?
And then what motivated you to be this competitive? Is it just your desire to win?
what motivated you to be competitive in the first place? ... why did you care?

common_preceding_contexts.description: After candidate shares a competitiveness anecdote

likely_reasoning.explanation: Ladders from observed behavior down to the root driver (winning, dopamine, mission, high standards).

canonical_questions.canonical_question: What is one niche topic you are unusually knowledgeable about (top 1%)?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: what is one niche topic that you are unusually knowledgeable about? ... What's your thing that you would say you're in the top 1%?
what is one niche and obscure topic that you are unusually knowledgeable about?
what are you in the top 1% of?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Frequently flagged as 'my last question'
common_preceding_contexts.frequency_estimate: very common as closing behavioral question

likely_reasoning.explanation: Surfaces capacity for deep, self-driven mastery and personality; interviewers often supply examples (anime card game, exoplanets) to prime the answer.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Are you an expert / in the top 1%? What have you done with this knowledge? (niche-depth verification)

representative_verbatim_variants.text: Would you say you're an expert? ... have you gambled before? Like do you put money on the line?
Do you think you're in the top 1%? Do you think you're an expert here?
What have you done with this knowledge? ... give me some examples of how you showcased this knowledge

common_preceding_contexts.description: Candidate names a niche interest, sometimes hedging

likely_reasoning.explanation: Pushes from a stated interest to concrete evidence (ratings, actions, output) to verify genuine expertise vs. casual interest.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Do you have any questions for me?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: All right, well, that's all the questions I have for you. Do you have any others from me?
that's it from my end. Do you have any questions for me?

common_preceding_contexts.description: After the final behavioral question (usually niche knowledge)

likely_reasoning.explanation: Standard close that also reveals what the candidate prioritizes and their genuine interest in the role.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: This role is five days a week in person in San Francisco — does that work for you?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: this role is five days a week in person in our San Francisco office. That sound good?
This role is five days a week in San Francisco. Does that sound good?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Often introduced as 'two final logistics questions'

likely_reasoning.explanation: Confirms a hard constraint of the role before advancing.

canonical_questions.canonical_question: Would you require visa sponsorship to work here?

representative_verbatim_variants.text: would you require visa sponsorship in order to work at workweave?
And would you require any sponsorship in order to work at weave?

common_preceding_contexts.description: Immediately after the 5-day in-person question

likely_reasoning.explanation: Routine eligibility screening; sometimes followed by clarifying probes on visa type (OPT/STEM/H1B/TN/O1).

example_verbatim_follow_ups: How's the weather?
Is it cold?
Where in Texas?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Location naturally seeds continued small talk (weather, specific city) to deepen rapport.

example_verbatim_follow_ups: And what do you do for fun?
What about your hobbies?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Interviewers consistently pivot from professional background to hobbies to assess personality/fit.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Candidate gives an abstract or high-level project description without a concrete hardest moment
example_verbatim_follow_ups: What was the hardest part about it?
What was the moment you realized this was harder than you thought?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Interviewers drill into the single hardest moment to verify genuine difficulty and problem-solving depth.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Candidate describes the project using 'we' or lists many components/team contributions
example_verbatim_follow_ups: What was your main contribution? What team were you on?
Were you the only person building this?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Collective framing prompts the interviewer to isolate the candidate's individual ownership.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Candidate describes a clever/architectural solution or makes a non-obvious technical claim
example_verbatim_follow_ups: What was the obvious solution and why is yours not obvious?
How did you come up with it?
Why use an external vector DB instead of AWS?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Probes reasoning/tradeoffs to test originality and, in stronger cases, to challenge claims for honesty.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Interviewer has confirmed (or not) prior YC exposure and proceeds to the signature question
example_verbatim_follow_ups: So you may already have an answer to this... can you tell me about a time you hacked a non-computer system?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: The YC-application check is a near-deterministic setup for the 'hacking a system' agency question.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Candidate is confused or draws a blank on the abstract 'hacking a system' question
example_verbatim_follow_ups: It doesn't need to be technical — maybe you hacked your sleep schedule (4.5 hours + nap)...
Some people put flyers on cars in concert parking lots...
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Interviewers commonly scaffold with concrete examples (sleep hacking, concert flyering, forging documents) to unblock the candidate.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Candidate gives a competitiveness story (especially a generic or harmonious one)
example_verbatim_follow_ups: What motivated you to be this competitive? Was it dopamine?
Why did you care?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Ladders from the anecdote to the root motivation; sometimes re-asked when the first answer is generic.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Candidate names a niche interest, often hedging on whether they are truly expert
example_verbatim_follow_ups: Would you say you're an expert?
What's your chess Elo / puzzle rating?
What have you done with this knowledge?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Interviewers push for concrete, verifiable evidence (ratings, actions, output) to test authenticity of the claimed depth.

follow_up_logic.trigger_condition: Candidate questions answered; interviewer transitions to logistics
example_verbatim_follow_ups: I have two final logistics questions. This role is five days a week in SF...
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Standard sequence: candidate Q&A then a paired logistics block.

example_verbatim_follow_ups: And would you require any visa sponsorship?
follow_up_logic.reasoning: Two logistics questions are almost always asked back-to-back as a closing screen.

edges.condition: Skip weather; go straight to intro
No YC setup; directly switch to behavioral

flowchart TD
    CQ001["Where calling from? (68%)"] --> CQ002["Weather small talk (41%)"]
    CQ001 --> CQ003["Tell me about yourself (68%)"]
    CQ002 --> CQ003
    CQ003 --> CQ004["Hobbies / fun (27%)"]
    CQ003 --> CQ005["What do you know about Weave? (18%)"]
    CQ003 --> CQ006["Why a startup? (20%)"]
    CQ004 --> CQ007["Hardest thing built (86%)"]
    CQ006 --> CQ007
    CQ005 --> CQ007
    CQ007 -->|abstract answer| CQ008["Hardest part / moment (41%)"]
    CQ007 -->|uses 'we'| CQ009["Your specific role? (16%)"]
    CQ007 -->|clever solution| CQ010["How/why this solution? (23%)"]
    CQ008 --> CQ011["Applied to YC? (39%)"]
    CQ009 --> CQ011
    CQ010 --> CQ011
    CQ007 -->|no YC setup| CQ012
    CQ011 -->|setup| CQ012["Hacked a system? (73%)"]
    CQ012 --> CQ013["Competitiveness cost (75%)"]
    CQ013 -->|generic answer| CQ014["Why competitive? (16%)"]
    CQ013 --> CQ015["Niche top-1% topic (59%)"]
    CQ014 --> CQ015
    CQ015 -->|hedge/claim| CQ016["Verify expertise (25%)"]
    CQ015 --> CQ017["Your questions? (80%)"]
    CQ016 --> CQ017
    CQ017 --> CQ018["5 days in SF? (64%)"]
    CQ018 --> CQ019["Visa sponsorship? (61%)"]

summary.top_level_interview_strategy: A highly standardized, lightly-technical screening call (~13-17 min) optimized for personality/founder-fit rather than deep coding skill. The interviewer (Prakul/Rakul, an engineer-recruiter at Weave/WorkWeave) follows a consistent funnel: rapport small talk -> self-intro/hobbies/motivation -> ONE technical 'hardest thing you built' deep dive (drilling for genuine difficulty and individual ownership) -> a battery of YC-derived behavioral questions (hacking a system, competitiveness cost, top-1% niche knowledge) -> candidate Q&A -> two logistics screens (5-day in-person SF, visa sponsorship). The interviewer explicitly frames most calls as 'getting to know you as a person.'
likely_core_question_sequence: Hey, how are you? Where are you calling in from?
Could you tell me a little about yourself?
Can you recall a time when you solved a technical problem with a clever, novel, or hacky solution?
Have you ever applied to YC before?

The reason I ask is that there's a question on the YC application we really like. It's the same question I'd like to ask you: Can you tell me about a time you hacked a non-computer system to your advantage?
Can you tell me about an area of your life outside of work where you are extremely competitive?
Can you describe the single most extreme instance of you being competitive in this particular thing?

Can you tell me about a niche or obscure non-technical topic that nobody knows about and nobody cares about, but you are an expert in? Meaning you're in the top 1% of knowledge about this non-technical topic?
most_common_questions: What is the hardest thing you've built (technically / outside of work)? (~86%)
Tell me about a time your competitiveness cost you something / most extreme thing to win (~75%)
Tell me about a time you hacked a non-computer system / broke the rules (~73%)
Can you tell me a bit about yourself? (~68%)
Where are you calling in from? (~68%)
What is one niche topic you're unusually knowledgeable about (top 1%)? (~59%)
Do you have any questions for me? (~80%)
5 days/week in-person SF + visa sponsorship logistics (~60-64%)
most_common_probe_triggers: Abstract/high-level project answer -> 'what was the hardest part / oh-shit moment'
Candidate uses 'we' or lists many components -> 'what was YOUR specific role?'
Candidate makes a non-obvious technical claim -> 'why not the obvious approach?' / direct challenge
Candidate names a niche interest (often hedging) -> 'are you top 1%? what have you done with it?'
Candidate gives a generic competitiveness story -> 'what motivated you / why did you care?'
Candidate stalls on the 'hacking a system' question -> interviewer supplies concrete scaffolding examples (sleep hacking, concert flyering)
signals_interviewer_prioritizes: Agency / resourcefulness / willingness to bend rules (the YC 'hacking a system' question)
Competitive drive and intensity, plus self-awareness of its costs
Genuine technical depth and individual ownership (not team-credit or buzzwords)
Intellectual curiosity / obsessive depth in some niche
Authentic, company-specific or startup-specific motivation (vs. money/comfort)
Honesty under challenge (notably in interview_010 and interview_050 where claims were stress-tested)
Logistical fit: in-person SF willingness and work authorization
surprising_patterns: The 'hardest thing you've built' is framed 'outside of work' in many later interviews, deliberately probing intrinsic motivation rather than just job competence.
The YC-application check (CQ011) is almost never an end in itself — it functions as a deterministic setup for the 'hacked a system' question.

Technical rigor varies dramatically: most calls accept the technical answer at face value, but a minority (interview_010, interview_050) become adversarial fact-checks, and in interview_010 the interviewer caught a candidate reading answers off a screen and ended early.

Several 'interviews' inverted the dynamic: candidates were actually founders/not job-seeking (interview_013, interview_040, interview_041), turning the call into mentorship/networking rather than evaluation.
Competitiveness is treated as a near-assumed trait ('if you want to work at a startup, you're competitive by nature'), which can lead the candidate toward a desired answer.
The closing logistics pair (5-day SF in-person + visa) is remarkably scripted and consistent across interviewers.
low_confidence_findings: Exact frequencies are estimates: 6 transcripts (interview_015, 021, 022, 025, 032, 037, 044, 046) were empty or near-empty, so percentages are computed against 44 usable transcripts but reported against 50 where noted — treat all percentages as approximate.
Whether the company-knowledge question (CQ005) is a deliberately retired part of the script or just interviewer-dependent is unclear; it concentrates in lower-numbered transcripts.
The motivation follow-up to competitiveness (CQ014) and ownership probe (CQ009) appear in fewer than ~16% of interviews and may be under-counted due to inconsistent extraction labeling.

Interviewer reasoning is inferred from question content and occasional explicit statements, not from stated rubrics (one transcript, interview_016, reveals a rubric: conscientiousness, agency, competitiveness, curiosity/production experience).
Stage boundaries and 'typical_position' are interpretive; some interviews reorder behavioral and technical blocks.
Two candidate names and several entities are transcription-approximate (ASR noise), so any verbatim quotes may differ slightly from intended phrasing.
